title: Bird Sound Identification Research
subtitle: Passive acoustic monitoring for avian conservation in Indonesia and Malaysia
reflection: I learned to fine-tune an established model (BirdNET) rather than training from scratch. The question is not always 'can I build this.' Sometimes it is 'should I.'
Spectrograms taught me that audio and vision pipelines are structurally similar. Sound is just images of time.
Running a research team means knowing when to step in and when to wait. I got that wrong more than once before I got it right.
I presented in front of ornithologists and signal processing researchers. Knowing the room matters as much as knowing the paper.

highlights: Took over as research lead with no existing dataset. First task: build it from scratch.
Sourced recordings of 219 species endemic or endangered in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Borneo: 12,511 recordings total.
Built the dataset from bird call recordings and negatives: noise, speech, and silence.
Split at recording level, not clip level, to prevent data leakage.
Achieved a 44.1% macro F1 improvement over the BirdNET v2.4 baseline on the endemic and endangered species subset, by fine-tuning on the 219-species dataset.
Field-validated at Bandung Zoo, Indonesia and Malaysia National Zoo: live animals, background noise, no controlled environment.
Paper accepted and published in IEEE Xplore. Presented at ICITACEE 2025, Semarang.

links.label: Paper: Bird Sound Identification in Indonesia and Malaysia (IEEE)

highlights: Overlapping bird calls in noisy field recordings remained the main failure case after field testing. Adding source separation to handle two species calling simultaneously over ambient noise.
Extending the paper with supervisors for PeerJ journal submission.
